title: Quiz: Superconducting Qubit Fabrication
description: Test your understanding of qubit fabrication processes, materials, and their impact on performance

questions.question: What is the Dolan bridge (shadow evaporation) technique used for?

options.text: Wire bonding the chip to the package

questions.explanation: The Dolan bridge technique uses angled evaporations through a suspended resist bridge to create the trilayer Al/AlOx/Al Josephson junction structure. The two aluminum layers overlap where the junction forms.

questions.question: How does increasing the oxidation time/pressure during junction fabrication affect the qubit?
questions.explanation: More oxidation creates a thicker AlOx barrier, which reduces the critical current I_c and thus the Josephson energy E_J (since E_J = h_bar*I_c/2e). Lower E_J means lower qubit frequency.

questions.question: What are two-level systems (TLS) in the context of superconducting qubits?

options.text: The |0> and |1> states of the qubit
Material defects that couple to qubits and cause decoherence
The two layers of aluminum in a junction
The ground and excited states of the resonator

questions.explanation: TLS are atomic-scale defects, particularly common in amorphous materials like the native oxide on surfaces and the AlOx junction barrier. They can absorb energy from the qubit, causing T1 decay, and their fluctuations cause dephasing.

questions.question: Sapphire substrates typically have lower TLS density than silicon substrates.
questions.explanation: True! Sapphire (crystalline Al2O3) has lower TLS density than silicon, which has a native SiO2 oxide layer that hosts many TLS. This is why sapphire is often preferred for the highest coherence qubits.

questions.question: The participation ratio p_i describes:

options.text: The fraction of Cooper pairs participating in transport
The fraction of electric field energy in a lossy material region
The success rate of the fabrication process
The coupling strength between qubit and resonator

questions.explanation: The participation ratio p_i is the fraction of the qubit's electric field energy stored in material region i. High participation in lossy regions (high loss tangent) leads to reduced coherence.

questions.question: If a fabrication process has 10% variation in junction critical current I_c, approximately what variation in qubit frequency should be expected?
questions.explanation: For a transmon, f ~ sqrt(E_J) ~ sqrt(I_c). Taking derivatives: df/f = (1/2)*dI_c/I_c. So 10% I_c variation gives ~5% frequency variation.

questions.question: Which techniques can be used to reduce TLS-related decoherence? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Using sapphire substrates instead of silicon
HF treatment to remove native oxides
3D cavity architectures to reduce surface participation

questions.explanation: TLS reduction strategies include: (a) sapphire has fewer TLS than Si, (b) HF removes the lossy native oxide, and (d) 3D cavities reduce electric field at surfaces. Increasing junction area (c) doesn't help with TLS and would change other qubit parameters.

questions.question: What is laser annealing used for in superconducting qubit fabrication?

options.text: Post-fabrication tuning of junction resistance to adjust qubit frequency
Bonding the chip to the package

questions.explanation: Laser annealing allows post-fabrication adjustment of junction properties by locally heating the junction, modifying the barrier. This 'trimming' can correct for fabrication variations and bring qubit frequencies to target values.
